Chunk # 0.
Isolation and Functional Assessment of Mitochondria from
Small Amounts of Mouse Brain Tissue
Christos Chinopoulos , Steven F Zhang , Bobby Thomas , Vadim Ten , and Anatoly A Starkov
Abstract
Recent discoveries have brought mitochondria functions in focus of the neuroscience research
community and greatly stimulated the demand for approaches to study mitochondria dysfunction
in neurodegenerative diseases Many mouse disease models have been generated, but studying
mitochondria isolated from individual mouse brain regions is a challenge because of small amount
of the available brain tissue Conventional techniques for isolation and purification of
mitochondria from mouse brain subregions, such as ventral midbrain, hippocampus, or striatum,
require pooling brain tissue from six to nine animals for a single mitochondrial preparation Working with pooled tissue significantly decreases the quality of data because of the time required
to dissect several brains It also greatly increases the labor intensity and the cost of experiments as
several animals are required per single data point We describe a method for isolation of brain mitochondria from mouse striata or other 7–12 mg
brain samples The method utilizes a refrigerated table-top microtube centrifuge, and produces
research grade quality mitochondria in amounts sufficient for performing multiple enzymatic and
functional assays, thereby eliminating the necessity for pooling mouse brain tissue We also
include a method of measuring ADP-ATP exchange rate as a function of mitochondrial membrane
potential ( ΔΨm) in small amounts of isolated mitochondria, adapted to a plate reader format Keywords
Adenine nucleotide translocase; Adenine nucleotide carrier; Systems biology
1 Introduction
The availability of a multitude of genetical mouse models of neurodegenerative diseases
greatly stimulated the demand for in vivo and in vitro approaches to study mouse brain
mitochondria functions Although the in vivo methodology to study mitochondrial functions
is not yet well developed, a wealth of in vitro approaches is readily available for in-depth
studies with isolated mitochondria However, such studies still represent a challenge because
of small amount of the available brain tissue, especially when a particular brain region is of
interest For example, dissected mouse striata weight only about ~12 mg (wet weight) If
using conventional techniques for isolation, purification and functional assessment of striatal
mitochondria, one has to pool brain tissue from six to nine animals for a single
mitochondrial preparation Tissue pooling has long been a routine practice in the field (e.g.,
where striata from ten mice had to be pooled per single experiment) (1) A disadvantage of
this experimental approach is that working with pooled tissue significantly decreases the
quality of the data  because of the time required to dissect several brains; it also greatly
increases the labor intensity and the cost of experiments as several animals are required per

brain mitochondria that allows us to isolate a workable amount of mitochondria from 7 to 12
mg brain samples, which eliminates the necessity for pooling mouse brain tissue One of most useful and frequently assessed mitochondria functional characteristics is their
oxidative phosphorylation Classically, it is evaluated by respirometry as the ratio of the
amount of phosphorylated ADP to the amount of the oxygen consumed (ADP:O ratio),
which characterizes the efficiency of oxidative phosphorylation and a degree of intactness of
mitochondria The amount of produced ATP is not measured in these experiments; however,
it can be evaluated separately by enzymatic assays Recently, we have developed a kinetic
assay to measure the efflux of ATP from mitochondria (2) This method allows one to
estimate ADP-ATP exchange rate mediated by the ANT in mitochondria, which is
influenced by many parameters (3), most steeply by mitochondrial membrane potential
(ΔΨm) (2, 4–7) We describe this methodology adapted to a small volume assay and using
commonly available instrumentation, such as a plate reader 2 Materials

Whatman paper 10.Wash bottle (~50 ml) filled with MSEGTA buffer (see Subheading 2.2, item 1) 11.A refrigerated microtube centrifuge (see Note 1) 12.A vacuum aspirator (see Note 2) 13.10–12 1.7 ml Eppendorf® tubes (see Note 3)

summary:
Summary 4:
Neuroscience research has highlighted the importance of studying mitochondria functions in the brain, particularly in relation to neurodegenerative diseases. To address the challenge of studying specific brain regions, techniques for isolating and purifying mitochondria have been developed. These techniques involve the use of surgical tools like scissors and forceps. Enzymatic and functional assays are then performed on the isolated mitochondria to assess their quality. One important aspect that is evaluated is the exchange rate of ADP-ATP, which is influenced by the mitochondrial membrane potential. Additionally, the efficiency of oxidative phosphorylation and the intactness of mitochondria are assessed. A kinetic assay has been developed to measure ATP efflux from mitochondria, which can be adapted to a plate reader format for small volume assays. This new method allows for the isolation of mitochondria from brain samples as small as 7-12 mg, eliminating the need to pool brain tissue from multiple animals.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. Use a "Microfuge 22R centrifuge" (Beckman-Coulter, USA) refrigerated table top centrifuge for the procedure.
2. The vacuum aspirator is optional, but a pipettor can be used instead.
3. Eppendorf® tubes can be substituted with any other brand of small polypropylene tubes with a conical bottom.
4. Use the monochromator-based Spectramax M5 plate reader (Molecular Devices, Sunnyvale, CA 94089, USA). If a monochromator-based plate reader is not available, use a filter-based plate reader with a 505/535 nm excitation/emission filter for MgG and a 495/585 nm excitation/emission filter for safranin O.
5. Prepare the following reagents:
   - MSEGTA buffer: 225 mM mannitol, 75 mM sucrose, 5 mM HEPES (pH 7.4), 1 mM EGTA, dissolved in water. Store at +0°C to +4°C.
   - MSEGTA-BSA: MSEGTA buffer supplemented with 0.2 mg/ml bovine serum albumin (BSA) essentially fatty acid-free. Store at +0°C to +4°C.
   - 100% Percoll™-MSEGTA buffer: 225 mM mannitol, 75 mM sucrose, 5 mM HEPES (pH 7.4), 1 mM EGTA, dissolved in 100% Percoll™. Store at +0°C to +4°C.
   - Experimental buffer: 8 mM KCl, 110 mM potassium gluconate, 10 mM NaCl, 10 mM Hepes (acid), 10 mM KH 2PO4, 0.005 mM EGTA, 10 mM mannitol, 1.5 mM MgCl 2, 0.5 mg/ml BSA essentially fatty acid-free, pH 7.25 (adjusted with KOH). Store at +4°C.
   - 50 mM diadenosine pentaphosphate (Ap5A), dissolved in water. Store at -20°C.
   - 2 mM magnesium green (MgG) 5 K+ salt (Invitrogen), dissolved in water. Store in 30 μl aliquots at -20°C.
   - 1 M K+ glutamate, pH 7.0, and 0.5 M K+ malate, dissolved in water, pH 7.0 (adjusted with KOH). Store at -20°C in 1 ml aliquots.
   - 200 mM K+ ADP, pH 6.3-6.9. Store at -20°C or preferably at -80°C in 0.2 ml aliquots.
   - 200 mM K+ ATP, pH 6.3-6.9. Store at -20°C or preferably at -80°C in 0.2 ml aliquots.
   - Solution of 0.5 M EDTA.
   - Solution of 1 M MgCl 2.
6. Use distilled (>17.4 M Ω resistance) water, such as Milli-Q, for preparing all water solutions and reagents.
7. If using an alternative buffer, determine the Kd values of ADP and ATP for Mg2+ and ensure experiments are performed at the same temperature. Use at least 1.5 mM MgCl 2 if 2 mM ADP is to be added.
8. Diadenosine pentaphosphate (Ap5A) stock does not require pH adjustment and can withstand at least ten freeze-thaw cycles.
9. Frozen stocks of mitochondrial substrates are stable for at least 1 year.

summary: Summary 5: The experiment requires a Microfuge 22R centrifuge (Beckman-Coulter) for sample separation and a monochromator-based Spectramax M5 plate reader (Molecular Devices) capable of measuring multiwavelength fluorescence and absorbance. Eppendorf® tubes can be substituted with any other brand of small polypropylene tubes. Various buffers and reagents are needed, including MSEGTA buffer (225 mM mannitol, 75 mM sucrose, 5 mM HEPES, 1 mM EGTA), MSEGTA-BSA (MSEGTA buffer supplemented with 0.2 mg/ml bovine serum albumin), 100% Percoll™-MSEGTA buffer (225 mM mannitol, 75 mM sucrose, 5 mM HEPES, 1 mM EGTA, dissolved in 100% Percoll™), and an experimental buffer (8 mM KCl, 110 mM potassium gluconate, 10 mM NaCl, 10 mM Hepes, 10 mM KH 2PO4, 0.005 mM EGTA, 10 mM mannitol, 1.5 mM MgCl 2, 0.5 mg/ml BSA). Diadenosine pentaphosphate (Ap5A) and magnesium green (MgG) 5 K+ salt (Invitrogen) are also used. The experiment requires specific solutions of 1 M K+ glutamate and 0.5 M K+ malate. Solutions of 0.5 M EDTA and 1 M MgCl 2 are needed as well. All water solutions and reagents are prepared using distilled water. Kd values of ADP and ATP for Mg2+ must be determined. The experiment requires a plate reader capable of measuring multiwavelength fluorescence and absorbance. The storage temperature for MSEGTA buffer is +0°C to +4°C. The storage temperature for the experimental buffer is +4°C. Diadenosine pentaphosphate (Ap5A) stock does not require pH adjustment and can withstand at least ten freeze-thaw cycles. The storage temperature for 1 M K+ glutamate and 0.5 M K+ malate is -20°C. The storage temperature for 200 mM K+ ADP and 200 mM K+ ATP is -20°C (or preferably at -80°C). The frozen stocks of the mitochondrial substrates are stable for at least 1 year.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Protocol:**

**Dissection of Mouse Striata for Mitochondrial Preparation:**
1. Sacrifice the mouse by a method approved at your Institution.
2. Decapitate with blunt-blunt tip straight Student Surgical Scissors.
3. Open the skull on the top with sharp-sharp tip straight Extra Fine Bonn Scissors, and carefully remove the brain from the skull. Take care not to damage the brain structure.
4. Place excised brain on the ice-chilled glass or plastic surface covered with wet Whatman paper. Wash out the blood out of the brain surface using mitochondria isolation buffer.
5. Using a razor blade, cut out the forebrain along the line a in Fig 1, and cut it in two halves.
6. Flip the cut piece of forebrain on its side. Hold the block with curved serrated tip Standard Pattern Forceps and gently work out the space between hippocampus and striatum lengthwise using Curved tip Dumont #7 Medical Forceps – Inox Standard Tip.
7. Flip out tissue flap.
8. Using the tips of Curved tip Dumont #7 Medical Forceps – Inox Standard Tip, gently separate the striatum from surrounding tissue and lift ("scoop") it out.
9. Immediately place it in 1 ml of ice-cold MSEGTA-BSA buffer.

**Isolation of Mitochondria:**
1. Homogenize brain tissue obtained in step 9 in Subheading 3.1 above in 1 ml MSEGTA-BSA with 2 ml Kontes™ Dounce homogenizer, tight pestle, with 30–35 strokes. Transfer into two 1.7 ml Eppendorf® tubes.
2. Add MSEGTA-BSA to the tubes up to the groove at the top (~1.7 ml, total volume), mix by pipetting or inversion.
3. Centrifuge at ~500 × g × 5 min.
4. Transfer the supernatant into clean 1.7 ml Eppendorf® tubes, centrifuge at 14,000 × g × 10 min.
5. While centrifuging at step 4, fill 1.7 ml Eppendorf® tube with 1 ml of 24% Percoll™-MSEGTA, and prepare 12% Percoll™-MSEGTA, 0.2 ml.
   - Take 0.1 ml of 24% Percoll™- MSEGTA and dilute with 0.1 ml MSEGTA.
6. Aspirate off the supernatant, resuspend both pellets (combine them) in 0.2 ml of 12% Percoll™-MSEGTA.
7. Carefully layer the suspension over the 24% Percoll™-MSEGTA solution by holding the tube at about 30 degrees, placing the tip of the pipettor into the tube's groove, and slowly releasing the suspension. Close the tube, turn it upright, and place it in ice before proceeding to the next step. Fill another 1.7 ml Eppendorf® tube with 1.2 ml of MSEGTA to use as a counterbalance in the centrifuge.
8. Centrifuge the prepared sample from step 7 at 18,000 × g × 15 min. After the centrifugation is completed, check the appearance of the sample. There should be an almost transparent band approximately in the middle of the tube, with cloudy top and bottom portions of the sample.
9. Aspirate off 0.7 ml of the top portion of the sample.
10. Add 1.2 ml MSEGTA, mix by inversion, and centrifuge at 18,000 × g × 5 min.

summary:
Summary (Iteration 3):
To determine the purified mitochondrial fraction, follow these steps: 
1. Set the plate reader acquisition rate to 0.33 Hz and excitation/emission wavelengths to 505/535 nm.
2. Ensure the incubator is set to 37°C and prewarm the plate and experimental buffer to the same temperature.
3. Use white opaque 96-well plates with flat-bottom wells.
4. Add 0.2 ml of the experimental buffer (minus the volume of the mitochondrial suspension) to a well, along with 2 μM magnesium green 5 K+ salt, 5 mM glutamate, 5 mM malate, and 50 μM Ap5A.
5. Add 20-40 μg of mitochondria to the well.
6. Record the baseline until steady.
7. Add 2 mM K+ ADP and record the first abrupt and second progressive drop in magnesium green fluorescence for at least 2 minutes.
8. At the end of the experiment, record the minimum fluorescence (Fmin) by adding 10 mM EDTA, followed by recording the maximum fluorescence (Fmax) elicited by the addition of 20 mM MgCl2.
9. Calculate the free Mg2+ concentration (Mg2+f) using the equation: Mg2+f = (Kd(F - Fmin)/(Fmax - F)) - 0.068 mM.
10. Use the BCA method to determine mitochondrial protein concentration.
11. To determine the ADP-ATP exchange rate, record the rate of ATP appearing in the medium after adding ADP to energized mitochondria. For de-energized mitochondria, record the rate of ATP appearing in the medium after adding ADP.
12. Convert [Mg2+]f to ADP-ATP exchange rate using the established method based on the differential affinity of ADP and ATP to Mg2+.
13. The bicinchoninic acid (BCA) method is used to determine the ADP-ATP exchange rate.
14. Magnesium Green Fluorescence (MSEGTA) is used to measure the extramitochondrial volume in isolated mitochondria.
15. Thermo Scientific is the company that provides the necessary materials for these experiments.
16. Fig 4a and Fig 4b show the results of the experiments related to the ADP-ATP exchange rate.
17. To determine the mitochondrial membrane potential, set the acquisition rate at 0.33 Hz and prewarm the plate and experimental buffer to 37°C.
18. Adjust the acquisition rate and prevent foam formation during the determination of the mitochondrial membrane potential.
19. Various notes provide additional details and considerations for the experiments.
20. Refer to Chinopoulos et al, Methods Mol Biol for detailed protocols.

Methods Mol Biol  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2013 April 16 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript8.At the end of the experiment, record maximum fluorescence elicited by addition of
1 μM SF 6847 3.6 Conversion of Safranin O Fluorescence to mV
Safranin O fluorescence can be “converted” to approximate values of ΔΨm expressed in
mV using the following three assumptions: (1) well-coupled mitochondria respiring on
glutamate plus malate, at pH 7.25, exhibit ΔΨm equal to −180 mV (2, 3) (see Note 14); (2)
in the same conditions, addition of ADP clamps ΔΨm to −145 mV for as long as there is
ADP in the well (2, 3); and (3) addition of 1 μM SF 6847 collapses ΔΨm to 0 mV From
these three points, the power function calibration can be constructed: f = y0 + a × xb, in
which safranin O fluorescence can be converted to mV The “ y0” constant of the power
function represents the residual safranin O fluorescence after mitochondria are fully
charged; “ x” is for safranin O raw fluorescence, “ a” and “ b” are constants and “ f” represents
calculated ΔΨm in mV (see Fig 4d) 3.7 Results
To assess the quality of mitochondria, we measured the respiration rates of the isolated
striatal brain mitochondria (bSm) The oxygen consumption was measured with Hansatech
oxytherm respirometry system (“Hansatech,” UK) in 0.25 ml of incubation buffer (125 mM
KCl, 2 mM KH 2PO4, 20 mM HEPES (pH 7.2), 1 mM Mg2+, 0.2 mg/ml BSA essentially
fatty acid-free, 0.2 mM EGTA; 5 mM α-ketoglutarate, 1 mM Na+-glutamate, 5 mM Na+-
pyruvate, 1 mM Na+-malate) Mitochondria were added at 0.1 mg/ml concentration We
found that mitochondria were well coupled and exhibited excellent phosphorylation
efficiency (Table 1 Note that ADP:O ratios >3 is due to the choice of oxidative substrates
that activate the so-called substrate phosphorylation carried out by the succinyl thiokinase in
the matrix of mitochondria, e.g., see (11)) Electron microphotography revealed normal appearance of isolated striatal mitochondria and
relatively low level of contaminating nonmitochondrial membranes (see Fig 3a) The purity
of the mitochondrial preparation was further assessed by immunoblotting (see Fig 3b) The
contamination by cytosol and unbroken nerve terminals (creatine kinase, cytosolic isoform
BB “BBCK,” marker) and that by endoplasmic reticulum (calnexin, marker) was very low
and comparable with that in a conventional whole brain mitochondria preparation obtained
by isopycnic centrifugation according to (12) 3.8 Conclusions
Overall, the method produces mitochondria of research grade quality in amounts sufficient
for performing multiple enzymatic and functional assays, thereby eliminating the necessity
for pooling mouse brain tissue Acknowledgments
This work was supported by the Országos Tudományos Kutatási Alapprogram-Nemzeti Kutatási és Technológiai
Hivatal (OTKA-NKTH) grant NF68294 and OTKA NNF78905 grant and Egeszsegügyi Tudományos Tanács
(ETT) grant to C.C., the NIH grant 5POAG014930-11 to A.A.S and NIH grants NS060885 and NS062165 to B.T 14If the mitochondria are uncoupled, either because of poor preparation, or for whatever reason they were impaired to begin with
(experimental tissue damage, genetic/pharmacological manipulation, etc.), they will exhibit a lower membrane potential In this case,
the assumption of a resting membrane potential of −180 mV must be abandoned, and a two-point (ADP effect and SF 6847 effect)
calibration of safranin O to mV conversion is advised A two-point calibration is not as accurate as the three-point calibration
elaborated above.Chinopoulos et al Page 7

1. Add 0.2 ml of the experimental buffer minus the volume of the mitochondrial suspension that is intended to be added, 5 μM of safranin O, 5 mM glutamate, 5 mM malate, and 50 μM Ap5A to a well.
2. Add 20–40 μg of mitochondria.
3. Record baseline until steady (usually a couple of minutes).
4. Add 2 mM of K+ ADP. Record for at least 2 min.
5. At the end of the experiment, record maximum fluorescence elicited by addition of 1 μM SF 6847.
6. Safranin O fluorescence can be "converted" to approximate values of ΔΨm expressed in mV using the following three assumptions: (1) well-coupled mitochondria respiring on glutamate plus malate, at pH 7.25, exhibit ΔΨm equal to -180 mV, (2) in the same conditions, addition of ADP clamps ΔΨm to -145 mV for as long as there is ADP in the well, and (3) addition of 1 μM SF 6847 collapses ΔΨm to 0 mV. From these three points, the power function calibration can be constructed: f = y0 + a × xb, in which safranin O fluorescence can be converted to mV. The "y0" constant of the power function represents the residual safranin O fluorescence after mitochondria are fully charged; "x" is for safranin O raw fluorescence, "a" and "b" are constants, and "f" represents calculated ΔΨm in mV.
7. To assess the quality of mitochondria, measure the respiration rates of the isolated striatal brain mitochondria (bSm). The oxygen consumption is measured with Hansatech oxytherm respirometry system in 0.25 ml of incubation buffer. The buffer contains 125 mM KCl, 2 mM KH2PO4, 20 mM HEPES (pH 7.2), 1 mM Mg2+, 0.2 mg/ml BSA essentially fatty acid-free, 0.2 mM EGTA, 5 mM α-ketoglutarate, 1 mM Na+-glutamate, 5 mM Na+-pyruvate, and 1 mM Na+-malate. Mitochondria are added at 0.1 mg/ml concentration.
8. The purity of the mitochondrial preparation is assessed by electron microphotography and immunoblotting. Electron microphotography reveals normal appearance of isolated striatal mitochondria and relatively low level of contaminating nonmitochondrial membranes. Immunoblotting shows low contamination by cytosol and unbroken nerve terminals and low contamination by endoplasmic reticulum.
9. Overall, the method produces mitochondria of research grade quality in amounts sufficient for performing multiple enzymatic and functional assays, eliminating the necessity for pooling mouse brain tissue.

The article provides information on the isolation of metabolically active mitochondria from rat brain and subregions using Percoll density gradient centrifugation. summary: Revised Summary: This article discusses the modulation of adenine nucleotide exchange by membrane potential and mathematical modeling of mitochondrial adenine nucleotide translocase. It also explores the regulation of intracellular free Mg2+ and contraction in cardiac myocytes, as well as the relationship between mitochondrial state and contraction. The rapid isolation of metabolically active mitochondria from rat brain using Percoll density gradient centrifugation is described. Additionally, the estimation of ATP release and changes in mitochondrial membrane potential upon ADP addition are examined. The yield and respiratory characteristics of isolated mouse striatal mitochondria are also discussed. The reconstitution of adenine nucleotide exchange and its modulation by membrane potential are investigated. Matrix substrate-level phosphorylation plays a critical role in the forward operation of adenine nucleotide translocase during F0F1-ATPase reversal. Immunoblot analysis of whole brain homogenate (H), whole brain mitochondria (M), and striatal mitochondria (sM) is performed. The role of calnexin (clnx) in the regulation of intracellular free Mg2+ and contraction in single adult mammalian cardiac myocytes is examined. Calculator programs for computing the composition of solutions containing multiple metals and ligands used in experiments with skinned muscle cells are utilized. Rapid isolation of metabolically active mitochondria from rat brain and subregions using Percoll density gradient centrifugation is described. Electron microphotograph and immunoblot analysis of isolated mouse striatal mitochondria (mCK and BBCK) are performed. Estimation of ATP production and membrane potential (ΔΨm) upon ADP addition in isolated mitochondria is conducted. The yield of striatal (bSm) mitochondria and their respiratory characteristics are measured. Relevant scientific publications include Biochemistry 1980; 19:556–560, Biophys J 2006; 90:423–432, Am J Physiol 1994; 266:C222–233, J Physiol 1996; 496(Pt 1):111–128, J Physiol (Paris) 1979; 75:463–505, and Faseb J 24:2405–2416, J Neurochem 1990; 55:698–707.
